The memory pointers that made me quit C
The memory pointers that made me quit
In learning C, control flow like

wasn't hard for me — I'd written game scripts before, the logic was close. I thought I was pretty smart, but then pointers came in and instantly sent me from heaven to hell. Didn't even get to take off before falling.
"The various control flow we just covered — everyone OK?" the teacher scanned the class, neutral tone.
The classroom was quiet, only scattered keyboard clicks. Common scene in computer class — most people are secretly gaming.
"On the midterm, there'll be a few questions on pointers!" the teacher added.
"Huh???" was the only word in my brain.
"Before explaining pointers, let's review last week's variable content — variables and pointers are related..." the teacher began.
When she said "midterm will have pointers", I panicked, only thinking:
"Crap, am I going to fail computer class?"
I had zero idea what a pointer was, so I poked the classmate next to me — the one I'd been playing Warcraft with.
He didn't even look up, still casting hero abilities, just casually: "What are you afraid of, you won't fail. Keep playing Nobunaga!"
I stared for 3 seconds — this guy was steadier than a wall.
So I... actually relaxed and kept clicking, raiding alongside him, as if midterms didn't exist.
In school, there's always that one annoying classmate — says "I didn't study" before every exam, then takes top 3.

Classmate (shrugs): "Aw, I didn't study this time, I'm going to bomb it."
Me (thinking): "Heh, I might finally beat them!"

Test handed back —
Classmate: ✨perfect score✨
Me: ……

Teacher (eyeing me): "You — come for makeup test after class."
Me: Σ(°△°|||)

Classmate: 😎 "I told you I didn't study."
Me: Orz

In the computer world, no matter what you declare — function, variable, array — they all end up in memory. Each storage location has a unique "address". C's pointers are tools for accessing and operating on these addresses.
How do you view a variable's address? Super simple — prefix the variable with
. Through it, you can directly get the variable's memory address.
int a = 10; // Declare an int variable a, assign 10
printf("Value of a: %d\n", a); // Output a's value
printf("Address of a: %p\n", &a); // Output a's address

prints memory addresses, usually as hex.
Strictest form:

Value of a: 10
Address of a: 0x16dd6eac8
's memory address. Note: your address won't necessarily match mine — memory allocation can vary per run.
For readers familiar with binary or hex,
is recognizable. Note: memory isn't actually stored in hex — addresses are just numbers, hex just displays them more compactly, so we use hex.
Because pointers let us directly operate on memory addresses. E.g., we can store
's address in a pointer variable, accessing
int *p = &a; // Declare a pointer variable p, store a's address
printf("Address p points to: %p\n", p); // Output p's pointed-to address
printf("Value p points to: %d\n", *p); // Use dereference (*) to get p's value

Value of a: 10
Address of a: 0x16f0c6ac8
Address p points to: 0x16f0c6ac8
Value p points to: 10
What's this mean? We can directly modify what
// Use dereference to modify what p points to
If you're a JavaScript developer, you might think "wait, isn't this just object / array references?" Yeah, conceptually similar.

The difference: JS references are like kids using safety scissors — worst case, you cut crooked. C pointers are a katana — one slip and you can wreck your program. So pointers are powerful, but use with extreme care.
Important: in C, to use something as a pointer, you must declare a pointer variable using
— the important part we haven't mentioned yet.
int p = a; // Declare an int variable p, assign a's value to p
int p = &a; // Declare an int variable p, assign a's address to p

main.c:4:9: error: incompatible pointer to integer conversion initializing 'int' with an expression of type 'int *'; remove & [-Wint-conversion]
    4 |     int p = &a;
      |         ^   ~~
1 error generated.
main.c:4:9: error: incompatible pointer to integer conversion initializing 'int' with an expression of type 'int *'; remove & [-Wint-conversion]
This is because addresses must be stored in pointer-type variables. We used
, but pointers are a special type, distinct from integers. We must use
Pointers were the Final Boss of C for me. The problem... I hadn't leveled up enough, didn't have my gear together, charged in to challenge — got one-shot.
In that moment I just whispered to myself: "Game Over… QQ".
Conclusion: even without talent, pay attention in class. Doesn't matter if you can't beat the final boss — at least don't get slaughtered by the slimes on the way.
Anyone else have those classmates who never studied but always aced exams in school?
